comment: Phase 10b exemplars — APPROVED 2026-07-15 (être reworked per review: teach usage and the avoir contrast, not etymology). These are the few-shot exemplars embedded in gen_conjugation.py / gen_grammar.py prompts; the rewrites ship as-is. Explanation schema: ordered sections [{header, body, bullets?, examples?: [{french, english}]}]. Word budgets: verbs <= 160 total, grammar 180-320 total.
voice_spec: Write like an expert teacher writing a reference their students actually read: direct, warm, plain. Explain WHY a rule exists or why French does it this way whenever there is a real reason — a learner who knows the why stops memorizing. Every sentence must teach something; if a sentence only encourages, delete it. Plain register: no hype metaphors (banned: weaponize, unfair advantage, VIP, superpower, game-changer, magic, secret weapon, hack), no cheerleading ('You've got this!', 'amazing'), no marketing rhythm. Address the learner as 'you'; contractions are fine; short sentences are fine. Concrete over abstract: show the form in a real sentence rather than describing it. It is fine to say something is hard or irregular — say it once, then show the pattern that tames it.

conj_etre.body: Être covers what someone is, how something is, and where things are. But French draws the line differently than English: age, hunger, thirst, heat, and cold all belong to avoir. Translating 'I am hungry' word for word is the number-one être mistake.

examples.french: Je suis fatigué. On est au café.
examples.english: I'm tired. We're at the café.
I'm hungry. I'm thirty. (avoir, not être)

conj_etre.body: Suis, es, est, sont do nearly all the spoken work. You already use est constantly — c'est is just ce + est, so every c'est sentence you've built was être practice. And on est handles 'we are'; nous sommes doesn't get said.

examples.french: C'est bon. Elle est là. Ils sont où ?
examples.english: It's good. She's here. Where are they?

conj_etre.body: Je suis compresses to j'suis, often down to one syllable — chuis. Tu es is nearly always t'es.

Reach for the imparfait first: states and descriptions are background, and c'était is how you review anything — a meal, a film, a weekend. The passé composé j'ai été exists, but mostly for one-off events.

examples.english: It was great. Where were you?

conj_attendre.body: Attendre is your first regular -re verb; entendre and perdre follow the same shape exactly. Drop the -re, then add -s, -s, nothing, -ez, -ent. In speech that means one sound — attend — covers je, tu, il, and on.

examples.french: j'attends, t'attends, il attend, on attend

conj_attendre.body: English waits FOR things; French waits them directly. Attendre takes its object with no preposition, and inserting a 'pour' is the classic English-speaker error.
Attends ! by itself is the universal 'hang on' — filler-level frequent between friends. The past is regular too: j'ai attendu.

gram_gender_articles.header: Learn the article as part of the word
gram_gender_articles.body: French nouns never appear naked — every noun carries le or la, and the article is where French stores gender. Learn le café and la rue as single units and gender never becomes a separate memorization project; it rides along for free.
Four words cover almost everything, and both definite articles collapse to l' before a vowel — that's why you hear l'eau and l'hôtel.
bullets: le / la — the (l' before a vowel: l'eau, l'école)
les — the, plural, both genders; des is the plural of un/une

gram_gender_articles.body: Gender looks arbitrary, but a word's ending predicts it with high reliability. These two rules alone cover thousands of words — including every -tion cognate you got for free: situation, solution, réservation are all la.
bullets: -age, -ment, -eau → le (le message, le moment, le gâteau)
exceptions exist (le problème, la plage) — collect them as they come, don't study them

gram_gender_articles.body: A wrong article almost never breaks understanding — un grande café still gets you a large coffee. French children get gender wrong for years and nobody stops them mid-sentence. Get it right where the ending tells you, shrug where it doesn't, and keep talking.

Fast French drops ne and swallows syllables, but the article survives everything — it is glued to the noun at any speed. That makes it a parsing cue: when speech blurs, the le or la you catch tells you a noun just went by.

gram_subjonctif_survival.body: After certain phrases — most importantly il faut que ('I/you/we have to') — French switches the next verb into a second set of forms called the subjunctive. Textbooks make this a mountain. In speech it is a toll booth: a handful of trigger phrases, and a handful of verbs whose subjunctive you will actually hear.

The subjunctive marks that an action is wanted, required, or doubted rather than stated as fact. Je sais qu'il vient — fact, normal form. Il faut qu'il vienne — requirement, subjunctive. You are not describing the world; you are pushing on it. That is the entire concept.

examples.french: Je sais qu'il vient. / Il faut qu'il vienne.
examples.english: I know he's coming. / He has to come.

gram_subjonctif_survival.body: For most verbs the subjunctive sounds identical to the present you already know (il faut que tu manges). Only a few very common verbs have audibly different forms — own these eight and you are covered:
bullets: être → sois / soit — faut que tu sois là
avoir → aies / ait — faut qu'il ait le temps
faire → fasse — faut qu'on fasse ça
pouvoir → puisse — pour que tu puisses venir
savoir → sache — faut que tu saches
prendre → prenne — faut que je prenne le train

gram_subjonctif_survival.header: One trigger does most of the work
gram_subjonctif_survival.body: Il faut que + subjunctive is the spoken workhorse for obligation — in conversation it largely replaces devoir. Learn it as a frame you drop verbs into, not as grammar to assemble.

examples.english: I've got to go. (the #1 way to leave anywhere)

gram_subjonctif_survival.body: The il drops — faut que j'aille, faut pas que t'oublies — and que elides to qu' before a vowel. If you keep one sentence from this lesson, keep faut que j'y aille; you will use it every day you are in France.

new_verb_paradigm_sample.street_notes: j'attends is already elided in formal French; tu attends → t'attends. Attends ! alone is the universal 'hang on'. attendre takes its object with no preposition — j'attends le bus, never 'pour'.

new_lesson_spec_sample.title: Survival subjunctive: il faut que + the forms you'll hear
focus: The concept in one line: wanted/required/doubted actions take a second verb form after que
il faut que as the workhorse trigger — the spoken replacement for devoir
The 8 audibly-different forms: sois/soit, aies/ait, aille, fasse, puisse, sache, vienne, prenne
Most verbs sound identical to the present — the subjunctive is smaller than it looks
Fixed survival frames: faut que j'y aille, faut qu'on parte, pour que tu puisses
new_lesson_spec_sample.street_angle: il drops (faut que j'aille), que elides (faut qu'on...); the whole structure is chunk-like in fast speech.
